Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The greens didn't move! Clicking on (16, 26) and (16, 8) didn't move any greens. The state is unchanged. This means my understanding of how to move greens is wrong.
Let me reconsider. Earlier I thought clicking adjacent to a green shows target diamonds, and clicking on those diamonds moves the green. But maybe the mechanism is different.
Let me go back to the beginning and carefully observe what happens when I click adjacent to a green cell. I need to understand the exact interaction model.
Actually, looking back at my earlier exploration, I did successfully move greens before. Let me check the history to see what actions actually moved greens.
Wait, I see the issue. When I clicked on (16, 26), I was clicking on a white cell in the grid, not on the target diamond. The target diamonds appear when I click adjacent to the green, and I need to click on the diamond itself, not on the grid cell.
Let me re-examine the target positions. When I clicked adjacent to green(16,14), the targets appeared at (15, 26), (16, 25), (16, 28), (18, 26). These are the diamond positions. The center of the diamond is (16, 26), but the actual clickable cells are the 4 diamond cells.
Let me try clicking on one of the diamond cells, like (16, 25) or (15, 26).

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me re-examine: click adjacent to green(16,14) to see targets, then click on a target
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 16, 'col': 15}])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After clicking adjacent to green(16,14):")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'g':
        boundary = n['boundary']
        min_r = min(p[0] for p in boundary)
        min_c = min(p[1] for p in boundary)
        max_r = max(p[0] for p in boundary)
        max_c = max(p[1] for p in boundary)
        print(f"  Target at center=({(min_r+max_r)//2},{(min_c+max_c)//2}) size=({max_r-min_r+1}x{max_c-min_c+1})")

# Now click on one of the target cells to move the green
# Try (16, 25) which is part of the diamond
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 16, 'col': 25}])
print(f"\nAfter clicking target - completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
greens = []
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        boundary = n['boundary']
        min_r = min(p[0] for p in boundary)
        min_c = min(p[1] for p in boundary)
        max_r = max(p[0] for p in boundary)
        max_c = max(p[1] for p in boundary)
        center = ((min_r+max_r)//2, (min_c+max_c)//2)
        greens.append(center)
greens.sort()
print(f"Current greens: {greens}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

  Target at center=(18,26) size=(1x2)
